questions.prompt: What is the interval from C4 up to E4?
questions.explanation: C up to E spans four half-steps (C–C♯–D–D♯–E), which is the definition of a major third. A minor third spans three half-steps; a perfect fourth spans five.

questions.prompt: Which two pitches form a tritone?
questions.explanation: F to B spans six half-steps (F–F♯–G–G♯–A–A♯–B), an augmented fourth — the tritone. C–G, D–A, and E–B are all perfect fifths (seven half-steps).

questions.prompt: How many sharps are in the key signature of E major?
choices: Five sharps (F♯, C♯, G♯, D♯, A♯)
questions.explanation: By the Circle of Fifths, E major has four sharps in the standard order F♯, C♯, G♯, D♯. Three sharps belongs to A major; five sharps belongs to B major.

questions.prompt: Which scale degree distinguishes the harmonic minor from the natural minor scale?
questions.explanation: Harmonic minor raises scale degree 7 by a half-step relative to natural minor, creating a leading tone a half-step below the tonic. This enables a strong V–i cadence. In A natural minor the 7th is G; in A harmonic minor it is G♯.

questions.prompt: On the alto clef, middle C (C4) is located on which line?
choices: The second line from the top
questions.explanation: The alto clef is a C clef centered so that its middle line equals middle C (C4). The tenor clef places middle C on the fourth line from the bottom. Violists read the alto clef as their primary clef.

questions.prompt: Which time signature represents simple quadruple meter?
questions.explanation: 4/4 has four beats per measure (quadruple) with each beat divisible into two equal parts (simple). 6/8 and 9/8 are compound meters; 3/4 is simple triple meter.

questions.prompt: In 6/8 time, how many beats are felt per measure when performed at a typical tempo?
choices: Six beats, each a sixteenth note
Two beats, each a dotted quarter note
Three beats, each a quarter note
Four beats, each an eighth note
questions.correctText: Two beats, each a dotted quarter note
questions.explanation: 6/8 is compound duple: two beats per measure, each beat a dotted quarter that subdivides into three eighth notes. Counting six eighths is the subdivision, not the beat.

questions.prompt: Syncopation is best described as:
choices: Accenting weak beats or weak parts of beats against the expected meter
Playing the same rhythm in two voices simultaneously
Adding ornaments to a melodic line
questions.correctText: Accenting weak beats or weak parts of beats against the expected meter
questions.explanation: Syncopation disrupts the regular metric pattern by emphasizing beats (or subdivisions) that are normally unaccented. It is central to ragtime, jazz, and many Romantic-era phrase rhythms.

questions.prompt: What is the fundamental difference between a tie and a slur?
choices: A tie connects two notes of the same pitch to sum their durations; a slur indicates legato performance across different pitches.
A tie indicates legato across different pitches; a slur sums durations of the same pitch.
A tie is only used in vocal music; a slur is only used in instrumental music.
A tie shortens the note duration; a slur lengthens it.
questions.correctText: A tie connects two notes of the same pitch to sum their durations; a slur indicates legato performance across different pitches.
questions.explanation: A tie joins two adjacent notes of identical pitch into one sustained duration. A slur curves over notes of differing pitch to indicate they should be played smoothly (legato), often in a single bow stroke or breath.

questions.prompt: In 9/8 time at a typical fast tempo, the meter is best classified as:
questions.explanation: 9/8 contains three beats per measure (triple), each beat a dotted quarter that divides into three eighths (compound). The classic example is the scherzo movement of many Romantic-era symphonies.

questions.prompt: Which triad quality is built from a root, a minor third, and a diminished fifth above the root?
questions.explanation: A diminished triad stacks a minor third (m3) plus another minor third, producing a diminished fifth between root and top. Example: B–D–F. A minor triad has a perfect fifth; an augmented triad has an augmented fifth.

questions.prompt: A C major triad with E in the bass is in what inversion?
questions.explanation: First inversion places the third of the chord (E for a C major triad) in the bass. Figured-bass symbol: 6 (or 6/3). Second inversion would place the fifth (G) in the bass with symbol 6/4.

questions.prompt: In the key of G major, what is the Roman numeral for the chord built on the scale degree A (the second scale degree)?
questions.explanation: In a major key, the chord built on scale degree 2 is naturally minor: A–C–E in G major. It is notated ii (lowercase). The supertonic in a minor key would be ii° (diminished).

questions.prompt: A dominant seventh chord in C major (V7) consists of which pitches?
questions.explanation: V7 in C major is built on G with the diatonic 7th from C major. Stacking G–B–D adds F (a minor 7th above G) to form the dominant seventh quality: major triad plus minor 7th. F♯ would make a major 7th chord, not V7.

questions.prompt: In four-part SATB writing, which voice-leading error is most strictly forbidden between two consecutive chords?
choices: Parallel thirds between soprano and alto
Parallel fifths or parallel octaves between any two voices
Contrary motion between soprano and bass
Repetition of a note in the same voice
questions.correctText: Parallel fifths or parallel octaves between any two voices
questions.explanation: Common-practice voice leading forbids parallel perfect fifths and parallel perfect octaves because they reduce the perceived independence of the voices, collapsing the texture into a single line. Parallel thirds and sixths are encouraged.

questions.prompt: A V–I cadence with both chords in root position and the tonic (1) in the soprano is called a:
questions.explanation: A PAC requires V–I, both in root position, with the tonic scale degree in the soprano of the final chord. It is the strongest tonal closure. An Imperfect Authentic Cadence (IAC) lacks one of those requirements.

questions.prompt: A V–vi progression at the end of a phrase produces what type of cadence?
questions.explanation: A deceptive cadence (also called interrupted) substitutes the submediant (vi) for the expected tonic after a dominant. The listener's expectation of resolution to I is denied, often used to extend a phrase. Beethoven uses this frequently.

questions.prompt: A non-chord tone approached by step and left by step in the same direction is best classified as a:
questions.explanation: A passing tone fills the space between two chord tones by stepwise motion in one direction (e.g., C–D–E over a C chord, where D is the PT). A neighbor tone steps away from a chord tone and returns to it.

questions.prompt: In SATB part writing, which doubling is normally preferred when voicing a root-position major triad?
questions.explanation: The root is the most stable tone of a root-position triad and is the preferred doubling in common-practice harmony. The leading tone is almost never doubled because both copies would need to resolve up by half-step, creating parallel octaves.

questions.prompt: In the key of C major, what pitches form the secondary dominant V/V?
questions.explanation: V/V tonicizes the dominant (G major). The dominant of G major is D major, spelled D–F♯–A. The chromatic F♯ raises scale degree 4 of C major and acts as the leading tone of G.

questions.prompt: From the key of C major, which of the following is NOT a closely related key?
questions.explanation: Closely related keys share all but one accidental with the home key — typically the relative minor, the dominant and its relative, and the subdominant and its relative. F♯ major has six sharps, distant from C major's zero. Modulations usually move first to closely related keys.

questions.prompt: Which chord function is described as predominant?
questions.explanation: Predominant (subdominant-function) chords prepare the dominant in a tonal progression. The most common are ii (and ii6) and IV. The standard tonal arc is T–PD–D–T: e.g., I–ii6–V–I or I–IV–V–I.

questions.prompt: A figured bass marked '6/4' beneath a bass note indicates which chord configuration?
choices: A root-position triad with an added sixth
A triad in second inversion, with the fifth in the bass and intervals of a sixth and fourth above
A seventh chord in first inversion
A dominant seventh chord with the third in the bass
questions.correctText: A triad in second inversion, with the fifth in the bass and intervals of a sixth and fourth above
questions.explanation: Figured bass numerals are intervals measured above the bass. 6/4 means a sixth and a fourth above the bass, which describes a triad in second inversion. The chord's fifth sits in the bass; the root is a fourth above and the third is a sixth above.

questions.prompt: The cadential 6/4 is correctly understood as:
choices: An independent tonic chord that resolves the dominant
A dominant chord with the 6th and 4th as accented non-chord tones that resolve down by step to the 5th and 3rd
A modulation to the relative minor
questions.correctText: A dominant chord with the 6th and 4th as accented non-chord tones that resolve down by step to the 5th and 3rd
questions.explanation: Although it looks like I in second inversion, the cadential 6/4 functions as an embellishment of V: the 6 resolves down to 5, the 4 resolves down to 3, over a stationary dominant bass. Roman-numeral analysis usually labels it V6/4–5/3 or simply V.

questions.prompt: Modal mixture (borrowed chords) typically involves:
choices: Borrowing chords from a closely related key
Borrowing chords from the parallel mode (e.g., using minor-mode chords in a major key)
Using chords built on chromatic mediants
Substituting V for I at a cadence
questions.correctText: Borrowing chords from the parallel mode (e.g., using minor-mode chords in a major key)
questions.explanation: Modal mixture borrows chords from the parallel mode, most often importing minor-mode chords into a major key (e.g., iv, ♭VI, ♭VII in C major). Schubert and Brahms use this expressively to color cadences.

questions.prompt: The Neapolitan chord (♭II) in C minor is spelled:
questions.explanation: The Neapolitan is a major triad built on the lowered second scale degree. In C minor, ♭2 is D♭; the triad D♭–F–A♭ is its root-position spelling. It nearly always appears in first inversion (N6) to place F in the bass, smoothing voice leading to V.

questions.prompt: An Italian augmented sixth chord (It+6) contains how many distinct pitches?
choices: Four distinct pitches with an added 4th
Four distinct pitches with an added 6th
questions.explanation: The It+6 is the three-note augmented-sixth chord — typically spelled ♭6, 1, and ♯4 (e.g., A♭–C–F♯ in C). The French (Fr+6) adds 2, and the German (Ger+6) adds ♭3, yielding four distinct pitches.

questions.prompt: Augmented-sixth chords most typically resolve to which chord?
questions.explanation: The defining feature of augmented-sixth chords is the augmented-sixth interval (♭6 to ♯4) that expands outward to an octave on scale degree 5, resolving to the dominant. The German augmented sixth typically passes through a cadential 6/4 to avoid parallel fifths.

questions.prompt: Which pair best illustrates a chromatic mediant relationship?
questions.explanation: Chromatic mediants are triads of the same quality (or related qualities) whose roots lie a third apart but which share only one common tone and require chromatic alteration. C major and E major share only E and are linked by a major-third root relationship. C–A minor is a diatonic mediant (relative minor).

questions.topic: Unit 7: Harmony, Voice Leading IV + Form
questions.prompt: A period in tonal music is most commonly defined as:
choices: A single phrase ending with an authentic cadence
An antecedent phrase ending on a weak cadence followed by a consequent phrase ending on a stronger cadence
A short motivic fragment repeated three times
questions.correctText: An antecedent phrase ending on a weak cadence followed by a consequent phrase ending on a stronger cadence
questions.explanation: A period pairs two phrases — antecedent (typically ending on a half cadence or IAC) and consequent (typically ending on a PAC). Both phrases often share opening material. Many Mozart and Haydn themes open this way.

questions.topic: Unit 7: Harmony, Voice Leading IV + Form
questions.prompt: Rounded binary form is best diagrammed as:
choices: ‖: A :‖‖: B A' :‖ — a return of A material within the second reprise
A B A with three independent sections
questions.correctText: ‖: A :‖‖: B A' :‖ — a return of A material within the second reprise
questions.explanation: Rounded binary keeps the two-reprise structure of binary form but inserts a return of A material partway through the second reprise. It is common in dance movements of the Baroque and in many Classical minuets. True ABA without repeats is ternary form.

questions.topic: Unit 7: Harmony, Voice Leading IV + Form
questions.prompt: In sonata-allegro form, the recapitulation typically presents the second theme group in which key?
choices: The dominant key (as in the exposition)
The tonic key, reconciling the tonal conflict introduced in the exposition
The relative minor of the dominant
questions.correctText: The tonic key, reconciling the tonal conflict introduced in the exposition
questions.explanation: The defining tonal narrative of sonata form is the move away from tonic in the exposition (second theme in dominant in a major-key sonata) followed by the return of that material in the tonic during the recapitulation. Haydn, Mozart, and Beethoven follow this scheme.

questions.topic: Unit 7: Harmony, Voice Leading IV + Form
questions.prompt: A sentence (in the Schoenbergian sense) is characterized by:
choices: A single 8-bar phrase with no internal subdivisions
A basic idea, its repetition (often varied), and a continuation that leads to a cadence — typically in a 2+2+4 grouping
An antecedent and consequent of equal length
Alternation between solo and tutti groups
questions.correctText: A basic idea, its repetition (often varied), and a continuation that leads to a cadence — typically in a 2+2+4 grouping
questions.explanation: The classical sentence structure (theorized by Arnold Schoenberg) presents a basic idea (m. 1-2), repeats it (m. 3-4), then continues with fragmentation and a cadence (m. 5-8). The opening of Beethoven's Piano Sonata Op. 2 No. 1 is a textbook sentence.

questions.topic: Unit 7: Harmony, Voice Leading IV + Form
questions.prompt: Rondo form is most characteristically organized as:
choices: Recurring refrain alternating with contrasting episodes — e.g., ABACA or ABACABA
Continuous variation over a ground bass
Strict imitation in three or more voices
questions.correctText: Recurring refrain alternating with contrasting episodes — e.g., ABACA or ABACABA
questions.explanation: Rondo form features a refrain (A) that returns multiple times between contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.). It is common as a finale in Classical-era multi-movement works. ABACABA is the seven-part rondo; sonata-rondo blends rondo with sonata principles.

questions.prompt: A texture consisting of a single melodic line with no accompaniment is called:
questions.explanation: Monophony is a single unaccompanied melodic line, even if performed by many voices in unison or at the octave. Gregorian chant is the canonical example. Homophony adds chordal accompaniment; polyphony combines multiple independent melodies.

questions.prompt: Heterophony is best described as:
choices: A texture with one melody supported by block chords
Two or more performers simultaneously presenting variants of the same melody
A texture of two or more independent melodic lines
A solo melody with a sustained drone
questions.correctText: Two or more performers simultaneously presenting variants of the same melody
questions.explanation: Heterophony arises when multiple performers play slightly different versions of the same line at the same time, common in many non-Western traditions and folk practices. Polyphony pairs independent melodies; homophony pairs melody with chordal accompaniment.

questions.prompt: A melody that moves predominantly by step is best described as:
questions.explanation: Conjunct motion moves by step (seconds), producing a smooth, singable line. Disjunct motion moves by leap (thirds or larger). Most Bach chorale soprano lines are predominantly conjunct, with leaps balanced by step-wise motion in the opposite direction.

questions.prompt: A melodic sequence occurs when:
choices: A motive is repeated immediately at a different pitch level along a consistent interval pattern
Two melodies are inverted relative to each other
A single note is sustained while the harmony changes underneath
Two voices alternate phrases in a call-and-response pattern
questions.correctText: A motive is repeated immediately at a different pitch level along a consistent interval pattern
questions.explanation: A sequence transposes a melodic (or harmonic) pattern to successive pitch levels, often by step. Sequences are central to Baroque development — Vivaldi and Bach use them constantly to expand material and modulate.

questions.prompt: In species counterpoint, first species is characterized by:
choices: Two notes against one in the counterpoint voice
Note-against-note motion between cantus firmus and counterpoint
Free use of dissonance on strong beats
Four notes against one in the counterpoint voice
questions.correctText: Note-against-note motion between cantus firmus and counterpoint
questions.explanation: First species sets one note in the counterpoint against each note of the cantus firmus, restricted to consonances (unison, third, fifth, sixth, octave). Higher species (second through fifth) introduce passing dissonance, suspensions, and rhythmic variety. Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum is the classic source.
